old_claim.note: Software Engineering provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: Cloud Computing & Distributed Systems is modeled as infrastructure and service capability; it does not require a separate Software Engineering node because cloud delivery depends on multiple disciplines, and software is one of many co-evolving artifacts within the platform stack.

source_shape.source_locator: NIST SP 800-145, 2011; sections defining cloud model and essential characteristics.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The NIST definition frames cloud computing by networked service access, pooled resources, multi-tenant abstractions, and service models; it does not present software-engineering method as a required prerequisite.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The official definition ties cloud technology to network access and infrastructure/software abstractions, not to a prerequisite discipline such as formal software engineering methodology.

ontology_before: Cloud Computing had an explicit software_engineering prerequisite, implying software engineering practices are required before the cloud node.
ontology_after: The cloud node now models broader cloud service infrastructure dependencies and retains only technology-level anchors that are directly evidenced, while software engineering remains available through its downstream child technologies.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: one prerequisite was removed because it was an over-broad contextual dependency. Preserved: cloud still depends on network access, virtualization, and early data center foundations.
would_reject_if: A standards text explicitly states software-engineering methodology is a required prerequisite distinct from cloud service infrastructure itself.
The cloud dependency graph is later narrowed so the node is only the software layer, with no shared infrastructure semantics.
short_reason: Software engineering is a contextual enabler, not a strict prerequisite for the cloud infrastructure+service paradigm.
